claims.claim_text: Air Canada's chatbot misled a customer about bereavement fares, and a 2024 tribunal held the airline responsible for the chatbot's false guidance.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a real legal incident with dated facts and needs a source, but the best detailed sources surfaced here are off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Zillow Offers shut down after large write-downs and losses tied to forecasting errors in its home-buying business.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, second paragraph
claims.rationale: The shutdown and loss/write-down details are real and best supported by Zillow investor materials, which are off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module gives an exact MMLU question count and says frontier models have entirely saturated it.
claims.span_hint: section: Evaluating Capabilities: Benchmarks, first Did You Know box
claims.anchor_text: The MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) dataset contains exactly 15,908 multiple-choice questions spanning 57 distinct academic subjects, which frontier models have now entirely saturated.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) benchmark spans 57 academic subjects, and frontier models now score so highly on it that it is less useful for separating the strongest systems.
claims.rationale: The exact count is not cleanly supported from the allowlisted passage I verified, and 'entirely saturated' overstates what the benchmark literature usually claims.

claims.claim_text: The module gives a precise MMLU score range and an exact MMLU-Pro question count.
claims.span_hint: section: Evaluating Capabilities: Benchmarks, paragraph after first Did You Know box
claims.anchor_text: However, it has become effectively saturated, consistently yielding scores exceeding 88-93% accuracy on frontier models. To combat this, researchers introduced MMLU-Pro, which contains 12,032 questions and expands the answer choices from four to ten, drastically reducing the impact of random statistical guessing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: However, it has become effectively saturated, with frontier models scoring above 90% on MMLU. To make the task harder, researchers introduced MMLU-Pro, which expands the answer choices from four to ten and focuses more on reasoning-heavy questions.
claims.rationale: The exact range and exact count are unnecessary false precision here, while the underlying benchmark progression can be stated more safely.

claims.claim_text: The module claims HLE was published in Nature in January 2026 by the Center for AI Safety because prior reasoning benchmarks had been exhausted.
claims.span_hint: section: Evaluating Capabilities: Benchmarks, second Did You Know box
claims.anchor_text: Humanity's Last Exam (HLE) was published in Nature in January 2026 by the Center for AI Safety specifically because frontier models had exhausted the difficulty of all previous reasoning benchmarks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Humanity's Last Exam (HLE) was introduced as a harder benchmark after older evaluation sets began to saturate.
claims.rationale: The publication details are real, but the causal phrasing overclaims beyond the narrower benchmark-saturation rationale stated in the source.

claims.claim_text: The Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard was retired on March 13, 2025 after evaluating over 13,000 models.
claims.span_hint: section: Evaluating Capabilities: Benchmarks, third Did You Know box
claims.rationale: The official retirement post explicitly gives the March 13, 2025 date and says the leaderboard had evaluated over 13K models.

claims.claim_text: Promptfoo was acquired by OpenAI on March 9, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Evaluating Capabilities: Benchmarks, fourth Did You Know box
claims.rationale: OpenAI's announcement directly states the date and acquisition.

claims.claim_text: The module says crowdsourced pairwise comparisons now dominate the evaluation landscape.
claims.span_hint: section: Evaluating Capabilities: Benchmarks, paragraph after Open LLM Leaderboard discussion
claims.anchor_text: Today, crowdsourced pairwise comparisons dominate the landscape via platforms like LMSYS Chatbot Arena, where humans blindly vote on output quality, forcing models to prove their alignment and reasoning dynamically.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Today, platforms such as LMSYS Chatbot Arena use crowdsourced pairwise comparisons in which humans blindly vote on output quality, giving teams a live complement to static benchmarks.
claims.rationale: Chatbot Arena supports the platform example and method, but 'dominate the landscape' and the causal gloss are broader than the cited paper establishes.

claims.claim_text: BLEU and ROUGE are reference-based overlap metrics that can correlate poorly with human judgments on creative or diverse generation tasks.
claims.span_hint: section: Traditional Metrics vs. LLM-as-a-Judge, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: G-Eval explicitly states that conventional reference-based metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE have relatively low correlation with human judgments, especially on creativity/diversity-sensitive tasks.

claims.claim_text: The module combines G-Eval and RAGAS into one claim about chain-of-thought scoring and very high correlation with expert judgments.
claims.span_hint: section: Traditional Metrics vs. LLM-as-a-Judge, second paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Frameworks like G-Eval or RAGAS use strict chain-of-thought prompting prior to scoring, achieving a phenomenally high correlation with expert human judgments.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Frameworks such as G-Eval and RAGAS use model-based evaluation to score qualities like coherence, faithfulness, and answer relevance, which can align better with human judgment than pure overlap metrics in some settings.
claims.rationale: G-Eval supports chain-of-thought and a reported correlation number, but the combined sentence overgeneralizes across frameworks and uses inflated wording.

claims.claim_text: Prompt injection is a major attack vector because system instructions and user input share the same natural-language channel.
claims.span_hint: section: Input Guardrails and Red Teaming, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: OWASP's prompt injection page directly explains the 'semantic gap' and the shared natural-language format of system prompts and user input.

claims.claim_text: The module states that benchmark contamination lets models memorize answers and overstates reasoning ability.
claims.span_hint: section: Evaluating Capabilities: Benchmarks, contamination paragraph
claims.anchor_text: If a model encounters the benchmark questions during its vast pre-training phase, it will effortlessly regurgitate the memorized answers during evaluation. This creates a false, highly dangerous impression of deep reasoning capability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If benchmark questions or close variants appear in pre-training data, evaluation scores can overstate a model's true reasoning ability.
claims.rationale: The contamination risk is real, but the current phrasing is rhetorically overstated and more precise than necessary.

claims.claim_text: Amazon scrapped a recruiting model in 2018 after it learned to penalize resumes with the word 'women's' and downgrade some all-women's colleges.
claims.span_hint: section: Responsible AI: Bias, Fairness and Interpretability, War Story paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a real, specific incident requiring citation, but the detailed sourcing surfaced here is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Demographic parity compares allocation rates across groups, while equalized odds compares error behavior conditional on the true label; a model can satisfy the former while failing the latter.
claims.span_hint: section: Equalized Odds vs Demographic Parity
claims.rationale: The metric definitions and distinction are standard and well documented, but the clearest source surfaced here is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module presents 'alignment tax' as a strong claim that making models safer severely degrades core reasoning and math ability.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Ignoring the Alignment Tax'
claims.anchor_text: Pushing models to be overly safe can severely degrade their core reasoning capabilities and mathematical logic (the alignment tax).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Safety tuning can introduce trade-offs with other objectives, so teams should monitor both safety and capability metrics during post-training.
claims.rationale: The current wording overstates a contested trade-off as a settled severe effect.

claims.claim_text: The module claims Kubernetes v1.32 or older inherently causes unacceptable guardrail latency and scale limits.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Using Legacy Kubernetes for Guardrails'
claims.anchor_text: Deploying low-latency guardrails on outdated infrastructure (v1.32 or older) causes unacceptable inference latency and scale limits.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Deploying guardrails on poorly tuned or undersized infrastructure can hurt latency and scalability, so teams should measure these systems under production-like load.
claims.rationale: This pins latency behavior to a Kubernetes minor-version boundary without evidence and should be rewritten as an engineering-performance concern instead.

further_reading.title: G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for the LLM-as-a-judge section and for grounding claims about overlap metrics versus model-based evaluation.

further_reading.title: OWASP Prompt Injection
further_reading.why_relevant: Good primary reference for the prompt-injection threat model and the system-prompt versus user-input boundary problem.

further_reading.title: Chatbot Arena: An Open Platform for Evaluating LLMs by Human Preference
further_reading.why_relevant: Grounds the discussion of pairwise human preference voting as a complement to static benchmark evaluation.

notes: Main allowlist gaps encountered: Nature for HLE publication details, legal reporting/tribunal coverage for Air Canada, Zillow investor relations for Zillow Offers, CNBC/Reuters-style reporting for Amazon's recruiting tool, and Fairlearn for fairness-metric exposition.
